1:
"What shall we say then? Shall we continue in sin, that grace may abound?" The witnesses note the antinomian objection raised and faced. On the question, the grammatical voices note the connection. Barnes and Robertson note Paul anticipates the perverse inference from 5:20 (where sin abounded, grace did much more abound): if grace increases where sin increases, shall we continue in sin that grace may abound the more?

On the objection, the witnesses note its antinomian character. The cavil twists the doctrine of grace into a license for sin — the very slander Paul faced (3:8). He raises it to refute it utterly (v.2).

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the purpose: having established justification by grace, Paul now turns to sanctification, showing that grace, far from licensing sin, destroys it. The verse displays the antinomian objection raised to be answered.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul anticipates the perverse inference from 5:20 — if grace abounds where sin abounds, shall we continue in sin that grace may abound? (Barnes, Robertson) — the antinomian cavil twisting grace into a license for sin (the slander of 3:8), raised to be refuted (v.2). Henry and Calvin note the turn from justification to sanctification: grace destroys sin, does not license it. The verse displays the antinomian objection. The verse marks the connection: the inference from 5:20. The verse displays the cavil: continue in sin for grace. The verse displays the purpose: raised to refute. The verse displays the turn: to sanctification. The antinomian objection displays the question Paul must answer to defend grace. The verse displays the perversity: grace abused for license. The verse displays the slander: grace as a cloak for sin. The verse displays the transition: justification to holiness. The antinomian objection — shall we continue in sin that grace may abound? — displays the perverse cavil that twists the doctrine of free grace into a license for sin, the very slander Paul faced. The verse displays the apostle raising the objection to refute it utterly, turning from the doctrine of justification by grace to the necessity of sanctification, and showing that grace, far from licensing sin, is the very power that destroys it. The verse opens the great argument of Romans 6 that the justified are dead to sin and alive to God.
2:
"God forbid. How shall we, that are dead to sin, live any longer therein?" The witnesses note the emphatic answer: dead to sin. On "God forbid," the grammatical voices note the rejection. Robertson notes the vehement denial (me genoito) of the antinomian inference.

On "dead to sin," the witnesses note the believer's state. Barnes and Calvin note the Christian is dead to sin — by union with Christ's death, sin's dominion is broken; the believer has died to its reign and power.

On "how shall we live any longer therein?" the witnesses note the incongruity. The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the absurdity: it is a contradiction for one dead to sin to live in it; the new life is incompatible with continuing in sin. The verse displays the emphatic answer: the believer dead to sin cannot live in it.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul vehemently rejects the cavil ("God forbid," me genoito, Robertson) — for the Christian is dead to sin (by union with Christ's death, sin's dominion broken, Barnes, Calvin) and how shall one dead to sin live any longer in it? The absurdity is plain (Calvin, Theodoret): it is a contradiction for the dead-to-sin to live in sin. The verse displays the emphatic answer. The verse marks the rejection: God forbid. The verse displays the state: dead to sin. The verse displays the incongruity: living in what one died to. The verse displays the absurdity: the contradiction. The emphatic answer displays the impossibility of continuing in sin. The verse displays the death: to sin's dominion. The verse displays the contradiction: dead yet living in it. The verse displays the new life: incompatible with sin. The emphatic answer — how shall we that are dead to sin live any longer therein? — displays the impossibility of continuing in sin for the believer: having died to sin's dominion through union with Christ's death, it is a flat contradiction to live in the very thing to which one has died. The verse displays the foundational principle of Christian sanctification, that the justified are dead to sin, and sets up its proof from baptism (vv.3-4).
3:
"Know ye not, that so many of us as were baptized into Jesus Christ were baptized into his death?" The witnesses note the appeal to baptism's meaning. On "baptized into Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the union. Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott note baptism signifies union with Christ, incorporation into him — "baptized unto Christ," the public proclamation of an inward spiritual relation (Robertson: Paul was not a sacramentarian; the union precedes the sign).

On "baptized into his death," the witnesses note the special relation. Baptism has particular reference to Christ's death — fellowship with his death, a death to sin corresponding to his death.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the substance: baptism is not a bare sign but the counterpart of Christ's sufferings; by it we are made partakers of his death, the old man put to death. The verse displays the appeal to baptism: baptized into Christ's death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: baptism signifies union with Christ, incorporation into him ("baptized unto Christ," the public proclamation of an inward relation, Robertson noting Paul was not a sacramentarian, Barnes, Ellicott), and particularly into his death — fellowship with his death, a death to sin. Cyril and Calvin note baptism is not a bare sign but the counterpart of Christ's sufferings, by which the old man is put to death. The verse displays the appeal to baptism. The verse marks the union: baptized into Christ. The verse displays the relation: into his death. The verse displays the fellowship: with Christ's death. The verse displays the substance: partaking of his death. The appeal to baptism displays the believer's union with Christ's death. The verse displays the incorporation: into Christ. The verse displays the death: shared with him. The verse displays the meaning: not a bare sign. The appeal to baptism — baptized into Jesus Christ, baptized into his death — displays the believer's union with Christ and particularly with his death: baptism proclaims and seals the inward reality that the Christian has died with Christ to sin. The verse displays the ground of sanctification in union with the crucified Christ, and sets up the burial and resurrection symbolism (v.4).
4:
"Therefore we are buried with him by baptism into death: that like as Christ was raised up from the dead by the glory of the Father, even so we also should walk in newness of life." The witnesses note baptism as burial and resurrection — and the immersion question. On "buried with him by baptism," the grammatical voices note the symbolism. Barnes, Clarke, and Wesley note the allusion to baptism by immersion — the body going under the water as a burial, picturing death to sin and Christ's burial.

On the mode, the witnesses divide on its bearing. Barnes carefully argues that though the allusion is probably to immersion, Paul's scope is not to prescribe the mode but to teach death to sin; the obligation arises from the fact of baptism, not the mode. Clarke and Wesley note the immersion allusion; Robertson notes a symbol is not the reality.

On "walk in newness of life," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the purpose: as Christ rose by the Father's glory (power), so the believer, raised with him, must walk in newness of life — the resurrection-side of baptism. The verse displays baptism as burial and resurrection, the call to newness of life.

The witnesses agree: we are buried with Christ by baptism into death (the allusion to immersion, the body going under as a burial, picturing death to sin and Christ's burial, Barnes, Clarke, Wesley) that, as Christ was raised by the Father's glory (power), we should walk in newness of life. On the mode they note the bearing: Barnes argues the scope is not to prescribe immersion but to teach death to sin (the obligation from the fact, not the mode); Calvin and Theodoret stress the purpose — the risen believer walking in newness of life. The verse displays baptism as burial and resurrection. The verse marks the burial: with Christ into death. The verse displays the resurrection: raised by the Father's glory. The verse displays the call: walk in newness of life. The verse displays the mode question: immersion's symbolism vs prescription. Baptism as burial and resurrection displays the two-fold call of death and new life. The verse displays the burial: death to sin. The verse displays the rising: newness of life. The verse displays the power: the Father's glory. Baptism as burial and resurrection — buried with Christ into death, raised to walk in newness of life — displays the two-fold reality of the Christian life: death to sin pictured in the burial, and new life pictured in the rising, as Christ rose by the Father's glory. The verse displays the believer's obligation to walk in newness of life, grounded in union with the dying and rising Christ, with the immersion symbolism vividly portraying the change. The verse continues the union-with-Christ argument (v.5).
5:
"For if we have been planted together in the likeness of his death, we shall be also in the likeness of his resurrection:" The witnesses note the organic union: planted together with Christ. On "planted together" (symphutoi), the grammatical voices note the imagery. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson note the word means grown together, united as a graft to a stock or seed sown together — an intimate, organic union with Christ, growing together with him.

On "likeness of his death... resurrection," the witnesses note the conformity. Ellicott and Robertson note the believer is conformed to Christ's death (dying to sin) and, certainly, to his resurrection (rising to new life) — the one guarantees the other.

The eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the secret union: not mere imitation but a real ingrafting, by which Christ's virtue is transferred to us — his death destroying our corruption, his resurrection renewing us. The verse displays the organic union: planted together in death and resurrection.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the believer is planted together (symphutoi, grown together, as a graft to a stock, an intimate organic union, Barnes, Clarke, Robertson) with Christ in the likeness of his death (dying to sin) and shall be also in the likeness of his resurrection (rising to new life), the one guaranteeing the other (Ellicott). Chrysostom and Calvin note not mere imitation but a real ingrafting, Christ's virtue transferred — his death destroying our corruption, his resurrection renewing us. The verse displays the organic union. The verse marks the imagery: planted together. The verse displays the conformity: death and resurrection. The verse displays the union: grown together with Christ. The verse displays the substance: not imitation but ingrafting. The organic union displays the believer's real participation in Christ's death and life. The verse displays the grafting: united to Christ. The verse displays the guarantee: death ensuring resurrection. The verse displays the renewal: Christ's virtue transferred. The organic union — planted together with Christ in the likeness of his death and resurrection — displays the believer's real participation in Christ: not a mere imitation of his example but an intimate ingrafting by which his death destroys our sin and his resurrection renews us to new life. The verse displays the certainty that those united to Christ's death shall share his risen life, and continues the explanation of the old man's death (v.6).
6:
"Knowing this, that our old man is crucified with him, that the body of sin might be destroyed, that henceforth we should not serve sin." The witnesses note the crucifixion of the old man. On "our old man," the grammatical voices note the meaning. Barnes, Clarke, and Calvin note "the old man" is the corrupt, sinful nature inherited from Adam — the whole depraved self, the native disposition before renewal (Ephesians 4:22; Colossians 3:9).

On "is crucified with him," the witnesses note the death. The old man is crucified with Christ — put to death, and (Barnes) crucified painfully, a lingering struggle by which sin is subdued; not at baptism but pictured there (Robertson), the believer's death to sin.

On "the body of sin destroyed... not serve sin," the witnesses note the purpose. The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note "the body of sin" is the whole mass of corruption (not the material body) — destroyed (rendered powerless, inoperative) that we should no longer be sin's slaves. The verse displays the old man crucified, the body of sin destroyed.

The witnesses agree: "our old man" is the corrupt, sinful nature inherited from Adam (the whole depraved self before renewal, Ephesians 4:22, Barnes, Clarke, Calvin), crucified with Christ — put to death (a painful, lingering subduing of sin, Barnes; pictured in baptism, not effected by it, Robertson) — that "the body of sin" (the whole mass of corruption, not the material body, Calvin, Theodoret) might be destroyed (rendered powerless), that we should no longer serve sin. The verse displays the old man crucified. The verse marks the old man: the corrupt nature. The verse displays the crucifixion: put to death with Christ. The verse displays the body of sin: the mass of corruption destroyed. The verse displays the purpose: no longer slaves of sin. The crucifixion of the old man displays the breaking of sin's dominion. The verse displays the death: the old self crucified. The verse displays the destruction: the body of sin rendered powerless. The verse displays the freedom: no longer sin's slaves. The crucifixion of the old man with Christ, that the body of sin might be destroyed, displays the breaking of sin's dominion in the believer: the corrupt nature inherited from Adam is put to death with Christ, the whole mass of corruption rendered powerless, that the Christian should no longer be the slave of sin. The verse displays the deep work of sanctification grounded in union with the crucified Christ, and continues with the freedom of the dead (v.7).
7:
"For he that is dead is freed from sin." The brief verse notes the principle: death frees from sin. The grammatical voices note the axiom. Barnes and Robertson note "he that is dead" (has died, with Christ) is freed (justified, acquitted, released) from sin — death dissolves sin's claim and dominion, as death ends a master's claim on a slave.

On "freed from sin," the witnesses note the meaning. Calvin and Theodoret note the believer who has died with Christ is set free from sin's bondage and guilt — released from its reign.

The witnesses note the application: as a dead man is beyond the reach of his former master, so the believer, dead with Christ, is freed from sin's dominion. The verse displays the principle: the dead are freed from sin.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: "he that is dead" (has died with Christ) is freed (released, acquitted) from sin — death dissolving sin's claim and dominion, as death ends a master's claim on a slave (Barnes, Robertson); the believer dead with Christ is set free from sin's bondage (Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays the principle: death frees from sin. The verse marks the axiom: the dead are freed. The verse displays the release: from sin's claim. The verse displays the image: death ending bondage. The verse displays the application: the believer freed. The principle displays the liberation that comes through death with Christ. The verse displays the dissolution: sin's claim ended. The verse displays the freedom: released from bondage. The verse displays the ground: death with Christ. The principle that he who is dead is freed from sin displays the liberation that comes through death with Christ: as death dissolves a master's claim on his slave, so the believer who has died with Christ is released from sin's dominion and bondage. The verse displays the legal and spiritual freedom secured by union with Christ's death, the ground of the believer's release from sin's reign, and continues to the believer's life with Christ (vv.8-10). The freedom is no legal fiction but the deepest reality of the new life, for the one who has died with Christ stands beyond the reach of the master that once held him in chains.
8:
"Now if we be dead with Christ, we believe that we shall also live with him:" The witnesses note the believer's life with Christ. On "dead with Christ," the grammatical voices note the union. Barnes and Robertson note the believer has died with Christ (to sin) — the foundation already laid (vv.2-7).

On "we shall also live with him," the witnesses note the consequence. As we died with Christ, so we believe we shall also live with him — both the present new life and the future eternal life, sharing his risen life.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the certainty: union with Christ's death guarantees union with his life; the believer who has died with him shall surely live with him. The verse displays the believer's life with Christ following death with him.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: if we be dead with Christ (to sin, the foundation laid, Barnes, Robertson), we believe we shall also live with him — both the present new life and the future eternal life, sharing his risen life; union with his death guaranteeing union with his life (Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays the believer's life with Christ. The verse marks the death: with Christ. The verse displays the consequence: life with him. The verse displays the certainty: death ensuring life. The verse displays the scope: present and eternal life. The believer's life with Christ displays the fruit of dying with him. The verse displays the union: in death and life. The verse displays the guarantee: death ensuring life. The verse displays the hope: living with Christ. The believer's life with Christ, following death with him, displays the certain fruit of union with the crucified and risen Lord: those who have died with Christ to sin shall surely live with him, sharing both his present new life and his future eternal glory. The verse displays the indissoluble link between dying and living with Christ, the ground of the believer's hope, and continues with Christ's death and life as the pattern (vv.9-10). This twofold union, in dying and in rising, is the very ground on which the apostle builds the hope of the saints and the certainty of their final glory with Christ.
9:
"Knowing that Christ being raised from the dead dieth no more; death hath no more dominion over him." The witnesses note Christ's endless risen life. On "raised from the dead dieth no more," the grammatical voices note the finality. Barnes and Robertson note Christ, once raised, dies no more — his resurrection life is endless, unlike Lazarus who would die again.

On "death hath no more dominion over him," the witnesses note the conquest. Death, which held Christ briefly, has no more power or claim over him — he has conquered it forever.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the bearing on the believer: as Christ's risen life is permanent and beyond death's dominion, so the believer's new life, united to him, is secure and abiding. The verse displays Christ's endless risen life, death's dominion broken.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ, once raised, dies no more (his resurrection life endless, unlike Lazarus who would die again, Barnes, Robertson); death has no more dominion over him — he has conquered it forever. Calvin and Theodoret note the bearing on the believer: as Christ's risen life is permanent and beyond death's dominion, so the believer's new life, united to him, is secure. The verse displays Christ's endless risen life. The verse marks the finality: dies no more. The verse displays the conquest: death's dominion broken. The verse displays the permanence: endless life. The verse displays the bearing: the believer's secure new life. Christ's endless risen life displays the permanence of the resurrection victory. The verse displays the finality: no second death. The verse displays the triumph: death conquered. The verse displays the security: the believer's abiding life. Christ's endless risen life, over which death has no more dominion, displays the permanence of his resurrection victory: having died once and risen, he dies no more, death's power over him forever broken. The verse displays the ground of the believer's secure and abiding new life, united to a Christ who lives forever, and explains the once-for-all character of his death (v.10). Because the Head lives forever, the members united to him share an indestructible life, secure against the death that once seemed to reign without rival.
10:
"For in that he died, he died unto sin once: but in that he liveth, he liveth unto God." The witnesses note Christ's once-for-all death and life unto God. On "he died unto sin once," the grammatical voices note the finality. Barnes and Robertson note Christ died unto sin once for all (ephapax) — a single, unrepeatable death, bearing sin once to put it away.

On "he liveth unto God," the witnesses note his risen life. Christ now lives unto God — his risen life wholly devoted to God, in unbroken communion and glory.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the pattern: as Christ died once to sin and now lives unto God, so the believer, united to him, is to be dead to sin once for all and to live unto God. The verse displays Christ's once-for-all death to sin and life unto God, the pattern for the believer.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Christ died unto sin once for all (ephapax, a single, unrepeatable death, bearing sin once to put it away, Barnes, Robertson) and now lives unto God (his risen life wholly devoted to God). Calvin and Theodoret note the pattern: as Christ died once to sin and lives unto God, so the believer, united to him, is to be dead to sin and to live unto God. The verse displays Christ's death and life as the pattern. The verse marks the death: unto sin, once. The verse displays the life: unto God. The verse displays the finality: once for all. The verse displays the pattern: for the believer. Christ's once-for-all death and life unto God display the model of the Christian life. The verse displays the singular death: to sin, never repeated. The verse displays the devoted life: unto God. The verse displays the union: the believer conformed. Christ's once-for-all death unto sin and his life unto God display the pattern for the believer: as Christ died once to sin to put it away and now lives wholly unto God, so the Christian, united to him, is to reckon himself dead to sin once for all and alive unto God. The verse displays the finished, unrepeatable character of Christ's death and the God-ward devotion of his risen life, the model the believer is now called to apply to himself (v.11).
11:
"Likewise reckon ye also yourselves to be dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God through Jesus Christ our Lord." The witnesses note the great application: reckon yourselves dead to sin, alive to God. On "reckon" (logizesthe), the grammatical voices note the act of faith. Barnes and Robertson note "reckon" means count, consider, regard as a settled fact — the believer is to reckon himself dead to sin and alive to God, applying Christ's pattern (v.10) to himself by faith.

On "dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God," the witnesses note the two-fold reckoning. As Christ died to sin and lives to God, so the believer is to count himself dead to sin's dominion and alive to God in newness of life.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note this is the first command of the chapter: to know and reckon the truth of our union with Christ; the reckoning is not pretense but faith laying hold on the reality. The verse displays the great application: reckon yourselves dead to sin, alive to God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the believer is to reckon (logizesthe, count, regard as a settled fact, by faith) himself dead to sin and alive to God through Christ — applying Christ's pattern (v.10) to himself (Barnes, Robertson). Henry and Calvin note this is the first command of the chapter: the reckoning is not pretense but faith laying hold on the reality of union with Christ. The verse displays the great application. The verse marks the act: reckon yourselves. The verse displays the two-fold reckoning: dead to sin, alive to God. The verse displays the ground: through Jesus Christ. The verse displays the faith: laying hold on the reality. The great application displays the believer's call to count the union with Christ true. The verse displays the reckoning: dead to sin. The verse displays the life: alive to God. The verse displays the means: through Christ. The great application — reckon yourselves dead indeed unto sin but alive unto God through Jesus Christ — displays the believer's call to lay hold by faith on the reality of union with Christ: as Christ died to sin and lives to God, so the Christian is to count himself dead to sin's dominion and alive to God. The verse displays the first imperative of the chapter, the reckoning of faith that translates the doctrine of union into daily life, and leads to the practical exhortations (vv.12-14).
12:
"Let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body, that ye should obey it in the lusts thereof." The witnesses note the first exhortation: let not sin reign. On "let not sin reign," the grammatical voices note the command. Barnes and Robertson note that since the believer is dead to sin (vv.2-11), he must not let sin reign (hold kingly dominion) in his mortal body — the exhortation following from the reckoning.

On "in your mortal body," the witnesses note the seat. Sin makes its assault through the mortal body and its appetites; the believer must not yield to its reign there.

On "obey it in the lusts thereof," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the meaning: not to obey sin's lusts — sin remains and tempts, but must not be obeyed; the believer is to refuse its dominion. The verse displays the exhortation: let not sin reign in the mortal body.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: since the believer is dead to sin (vv.2-11), he must not let sin reign (hold kingly dominion, Barnes, Robertson) in his mortal body — the seat of sin's assault through its appetites — nor obey its lusts; sin remains and tempts but must not be obeyed (Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays the first exhortation. The verse marks the command: let not sin reign. The verse displays the seat: the mortal body. The verse displays the refusal: not to obey its lusts. The verse displays the basis: dead to sin. The exhortation displays the believer's active resistance to sin's dominion. The verse displays the prohibition: sin not to reign. The verse displays the body: the field of conflict. The verse displays the obedience refused: sin's lusts denied. The first exhortation — let not sin reign in your mortal body — displays the believer's active duty to resist sin's dominion: since dead to sin, the Christian must not allow sin to hold kingly sway in the mortal body nor obey its lusts. The verse displays the practical outworking of the reckoning of faith, the refusal to let the dethroned sin regain its reign, and continues with the yielding of the members (v.13).
13:
"Neither yield ye your members as instruments of unrighteousness unto sin: but yield yourselves unto God, as those that are alive from the dead, and your members as instruments of righteousness unto God." The witnesses note the exhortation to yield to God. On "yield not your members... unto sin," the grammatical voices note the prohibition. Barnes and Robertson note "members" are the bodily faculties; "instruments" (hopla) can mean weapons — not to present them to sin as weapons of unrighteousness.

On "yield yourselves unto God," the witnesses note the positive surrender. Instead, the believer is to present himself wholly to God, as one alive from the dead, and his members as weapons (instruments) of righteousness.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the decisive consecration: the whole self and all its faculties surrendered to God's service — the positive counterpart to refusing sin. The verse displays the exhortation: yield not to sin but yourselves to God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the believer is not to yield his members (bodily faculties) as instruments (hopla, weapons) of unrighteousness unto sin, but to yield himself wholly unto God, as one alive from the dead, and his members as instruments of righteousness (Barnes, Robertson). Calvin and Theodoret note the decisive consecration: the whole self surrendered to God's service. The verse displays the exhortation to yield to God. The verse marks the prohibition: members not to sin. The verse displays the surrender: yourselves to God. The verse displays the image: weapons of righteousness. The verse displays the ground: alive from the dead. The exhortation displays the believer's whole-self consecration to God. The verse displays the refusal: members not weapons of sin. The verse displays the dedication: to God's service. The verse displays the standing: alive from the dead. The exhortation to yield not to sin but yourselves to God displays the believer's call to whole-self consecration: not presenting the bodily faculties as weapons of unrighteousness, but surrendering self and members to God as instruments of righteousness, as those alive from the dead. The verse displays the decisive dedication that follows the reckoning of faith, the positive devotion of the whole man to God, and grounds it in the promise of grace (v.14).
14:
"For sin shall not have dominion over you: for ye are not under the law, but under grace." The witnesses note the promise: sin shall not have dominion. On "sin shall not have dominion," the grammatical voices note the assurance. The Reformed Gill notes this is not a mere exhortation but an absolute promise: sin shall not lord it over the believer — not its damning power only but its governing, tyrannical power broken.

On "not under the law, but under grace," the witnesses debate the meaning. Barnes, Calvin, and Wesley note "under the law" means under the law as a covenant of works/legalism (which exposes sin but gives no power), while "under grace" is the dispensation of mercy that both pardons and gives power by the Spirit to conquer sin. Theodoret notes grace adds help where the law only commanded.

On the connection, the witnesses note the ground: sin shall not reign because the believer is under grace, which supplies the Spirit's power. The verse displays the promise: sin shall not have dominion, for ye are under grace.

The witnesses agree: sin shall not have dominion over the believer — Gill noting an absolute promise, not a mere exhortation (sin's tyrannical, governing power broken) — for the believer is not under the law but under grace. They explain "under the law" as the covenant of works/legalism (which exposes sin but gives no power) and "under grace" as the dispensation of mercy that pardons and gives power by the Spirit (Barnes, Calvin, Wesley); Theodoret notes grace adds help where the law only commanded. The verse displays the promise. The verse marks the assurance: sin shall not have dominion. The verse displays the ground: under grace, not law. The verse displays the contrast: law exposes, grace empowers. The verse displays the help: the Spirit's power. The promise displays the certainty of victory over sin under grace. The verse displays the promise: sin not lording it. The verse displays the freedom: from the law's rigor. The verse displays the power: grace supplying the Spirit. The promise that sin shall not have dominion, for the believer is under grace, displays the certainty of victory over sin: not under the law (which exposes sin but gives no power to conquer it) but under grace (which both pardons and supplies the Spirit's power), the believer is assured that sin's tyrannical reign is broken. The verse displays the great difference between law and grace as to the conquest of sin, and sets up the next objection (v.15).
15:
"What then? shall we sin, because we are not under the law, but under grace? God forbid." The witnesses note the renewed antinomian objection. On "shall we sin because under grace?" the grammatical voices note the cavil. Barnes and Robertson note Paul anticipates the misuse of v.14: if we are under grace and not law, may we then sin freely? — the same antinomian abuse in a new form.

On "God forbid," the witnesses note the rejection. Robertson notes the vehement denial (me genoito) — being under grace is no license to sin, but the very opposite.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the answer to come: grace, far from licensing sin, makes us servants of righteousness (vv.16-18). The verse displays the renewed objection rejected.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul anticipates the misuse of v.14 — if under grace and not law, may we sin freely? (the antinomian abuse in a new form, Barnes, Robertson) — and vehemently rejects it ("God forbid," me genoito); being under grace is no license to sin but the opposite. Calvin and Theodoret note the coming answer: grace makes us servants of righteousness (vv.16-18). The verse displays the renewed objection rejected. The verse marks the cavil: sin because under grace? The verse displays the rejection: God forbid. The verse displays the abuse: grace as license. The verse displays the answer to come: servants of righteousness. The renewed objection displays the persistent antinomian misuse of grace. The verse displays the cavil: license under grace. The verse displays the denial: God forbid. The verse displays the truth: grace opposes sin. The renewed antinomian objection — shall we sin because under grace? — and Paul's emphatic rejection display the persistent misuse of free grace as a license for sin, which the apostle once more utterly denies. The verse displays that being under grace, far from permitting sin, binds the believer to righteousness, and sets up the argument from servitude (vv.16-18). The verse marks Paul's unwavering opposition to antinomianism. Far from loosening the obligation to holiness, the reign of grace binds the believer more closely to righteousness than the law ever could, for love constrains where command alone could not.
16:
"Know ye not, that to whom ye yield yourselves servants to obey, his servants ye are to whom ye obey; whether of sin unto death, or of obedience unto righteousness?" The witnesses note the principle of servitude. On "to whom ye yield... his servants ye are," the grammatical voices note the axiom. Barnes and Robertson note the principle: whomever one obeys, that one is his master; obedience determines servitude — one is the slave of whatever he serves.

On "of sin unto death, or of obedience unto righteousness," the witnesses note the two masters. There are only two services: sin (leading to death) or obedience (leading to righteousness). No neutral ground; every person serves one or the other.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the application: since obedience determines mastery, the one under grace who obeys God is God's servant, not sin's — so grace is no license to sin. The verse displays the principle: one is the servant of whom he obeys.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the principle is that whomever one yields to and obeys, that one is his master — obedience determines servitude (Barnes, Robertson) — and there are only two services: sin unto death or obedience unto righteousness, no neutral ground. Henry and Calvin note the application: the one under grace who obeys God is God's servant, not sin's, so grace licenses no sin. The verse displays the principle of servitude. The verse marks the axiom: servants of whom we obey. The verse displays the two masters: sin or obedience. The verse displays the ends: death or righteousness. The verse displays the application: no neutral ground. The principle of servitude displays the impossibility of serving both sin and God. The verse displays the mastery: determined by obedience. The verse displays the alternatives: sin or righteousness. The verse displays the exclusion: no middle service. The principle of servitude — that one is the servant of whom he obeys, whether sin unto death or obedience unto righteousness — displays the impossibility of serving both sin and God: since obedience determines mastery, the believer who obeys God is God's servant, not sin's, and grace therefore licenses no sin. The verse displays the either/or of spiritual servitude, the ground for the apostle's confidence that the believer serves righteousness, and continues with thanksgiving for the believers' changed service (v.17).
17:
"But God be thanked, that ye were the servants of sin, but ye have obeyed from the heart that form of doctrine which was delivered you." The witnesses note thanksgiving for the believers' changed service. On "ye were the servants of sin," the grammatical voices note the past state. Barnes and Robertson note the believers were once slaves of sin — their former condition, now happily past.

On "obeyed from the heart," the witnesses note the genuine conversion. They have obeyed from the heart (sincerely, inwardly, not merely outwardly) — a heart-obedience marking true conversion.

On "that form of doctrine delivered you," the witnesses note the gospel mold. Robertson and Calvin note "form" (tupos) means pattern or mold — the believers were as it were poured into the mold of the gospel teaching, shaped by it; the thanksgiving is to God who wrought this change. The verse displays thanksgiving for the believers' heart-obedience to the gospel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the believers were once servants of sin (their former state, now past, Barnes, Robertson), but have obeyed from the heart (sincerely, inwardly, marking true conversion) that form of doctrine (tupos, the mold or pattern of the gospel) into which they were delivered/shaped (Robertson, Calvin). The thanksgiving is to God who wrought the change. The verse displays thanksgiving for the changed service. The verse marks the past: servants of sin. The verse displays the change: obeyed from the heart. The verse displays the mold: the form of doctrine. The verse displays the thanks: to God who changed them. The thanksgiving displays the gracious transformation from sin's service to God's. The verse displays the former bondage: servants of sin. The verse displays the heart-obedience: sincere conversion. The verse displays the gospel mold: shaping the believer. Thanksgiving for the believers' heart-obedience to the gospel displays the gracious transformation from sin's service to God's: once slaves of sin, they have now, by God's grace, obeyed from the heart the pattern of gospel doctrine into which they were delivered. The verse displays the genuineness of their conversion (a heart-obedience, not mere outward conformity) and the apostle's thankfulness to God who wrought it, and states the result (v.18).
18:
"Being then made free from sin, ye became the servants of righteousness." The brief verse notes the result: freed from sin, servants of righteousness. The grammatical voices note the exchange of masters. Barnes and Robertson note the believers, made free from sin (its bondage and dominion), became servants of righteousness — not freedom into autonomy but a change of service.

On "servants of righteousness," the witnesses note the new bondage. Christian freedom is not lawlessness but a glad servitude to righteousness — freed from one master only to serve a better.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the paradox: true freedom is the service of righteousness; the believer exchanges the tyranny of sin for the blessed servitude of God. The verse displays the result: freed from sin, servants of righteousness.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the believers, made free from sin (its bondage and dominion), became servants of righteousness — not freedom into autonomy but a change of masters (Barnes, Robertson); Christian freedom is not lawlessness but a glad servitude to righteousness. Calvin and Theodoret note the paradox: true freedom is the service of righteousness, the tyranny of sin exchanged for the blessed servitude of God. The verse displays the result. The verse marks the freedom: from sin. The verse displays the new service: of righteousness. The verse displays the exchange: of masters. The verse displays the paradox: freedom as servitude. The result displays the nature of Christian freedom as the service of righteousness. The verse displays the liberation: from sin's bondage. The verse displays the bondage: to righteousness. The verse displays the truth: freedom is service. The result — freed from sin, ye became servants of righteousness — displays the true nature of Christian freedom: not autonomy or lawlessness, but a glad exchange of masters, the tyranny of sin for the blessed servitude of righteousness. The verse displays the paradox that the believer's liberty is found in the service of God, and continues the slave-imagery exhortation (v.19). This blessed exchange of masters is the very heart of the transforming work of the gospel, turning the slave of corruption into the willing servant of the living God.
19:
"I speak after the manner of men because of the infirmity of your flesh: for as ye have yielded your members servants to uncleanness and to iniquity unto iniquity; even so now yield your members servants to righteousness unto holiness." The witnesses note the exhortation drawn from the slave-figure. On "I speak after the manner of men," the grammatical voices note the apology. Barnes, Chrysostom, and Robertson note Paul apologizes for the slave-metaphor — using a human illustration (servitude) because of their weakness, to make the truth plain and vivid.

On the contrast, the witnesses note the parallel. As they once yielded their members to uncleanness and iniquity (progressing to more iniquity), so now they are to yield them to righteousness unto holiness — the same wholeness of devotion, now to a holy end.

On "unto holiness," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the goal: the service of righteousness issues in holiness (sanctification) — the progressive growth in holiness as the fruit. The verse displays the exhortation: yield your members to righteousness unto holiness.

The witnesses agree: Paul apologizes for the slave-metaphor (speaking "after the manner of men" because of their weakness, to make the truth vivid, Barnes, Chrysostom, Robertson), drawing the contrast — as they once yielded their members to uncleanness and iniquity (progressing to more iniquity), so now they are to yield them to righteousness unto holiness, the same wholeness of devotion now to a holy end. Calvin and Theodoret note the goal: the service of righteousness issuing in holiness (sanctification). The verse displays the exhortation. The verse marks the apology: speaking humanly. The verse displays the contrast: former and new service. The verse displays the goal: unto holiness. The verse displays the wholeness: full devotion. The exhortation displays the call to as full a devotion to righteousness as once to sin. The verse displays the parallel: members yielded. The verse displays the progress: unto holiness. The verse displays the wholeness: the same zeal. The exhortation to yield the members to righteousness unto holiness displays the call to as full and zealous a devotion to righteousness as once was given to sin: as the members were once instruments of mounting iniquity, so now they are to be wholly devoted to righteousness, issuing in progressive holiness. The verse displays the apostle's vivid use of the slave-figure to press the duty of complete consecration, and contrasts the two services and their ends (vv.20-22).
20:
"For when ye were the servants of sin, ye were free from righteousness." The brief verse notes the former state: free from righteousness. The grammatical voices note the irony. Barnes and Robertson note that when they were slaves of sin, they were "free" from righteousness — a bitter freedom, untouched by and uncontrolled by righteousness.

On the meaning, the witnesses note the bondage. This "freedom" from righteousness was no true liberty but the worst bondage — wholly given to sin, with no claim of righteousness upon them.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the purpose: Paul recalls their former state to set off the shamefulness of sin's service and the blessedness of the new. The verse displays the former state: servants of sin, free from righteousness.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: when they were slaves of sin, they were "free" from righteousness (a bitter, ironic freedom — untouched and uncontrolled by righteousness, Barnes, Robertson); no true liberty but the worst bondage, wholly given to sin. Calvin and Theodoret note Paul recalls their former state to set off the shamefulness of sin's service. The verse displays the former state. The verse marks the irony: free from righteousness. The verse displays the bondage: slaves of sin. The verse displays the emptiness: no righteousness. The verse displays the purpose: to shame sin's service. The former state displays the bitter "freedom" of sin's slavery. The verse displays the irony: free from righteousness. The verse displays the slavery: to sin. The verse displays the contrast: with the new service. The former state — when servants of sin, free from righteousness — displays the bitter irony of sin's slavery: the only "freedom" it gives is freedom from righteousness, a bondage in which no claim of righteousness governs the life. The verse displays the shamefulness of the old service, recalled to magnify the blessedness of the new, and presses the question of its fruit (v.21). Such was the dreadful liberty of the old estate, a freedom only to perish, which the apostle recalls that the believer might shrink the more from any return to its service.
21:
"What fruit had ye then in those things whereof ye are now ashamed? for the end of those things is death." The witnesses note the worthless fruit of sin, ending in death. On "what fruit had ye then?" the grammatical voices note the challenge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks what profit they gained from their former sins — the answer being none worth having, only what they are now ashamed of.

On "whereof ye are now ashamed," the witnesses note the shame. The enlightened conscience now blushes at the former sins — shame the only present harvest.

On "the end of those things is death," the puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the terminus: the final issue of those sins is death (eternal ruin) — sin's fruit shameful now, deadly at the last. The verse displays the worthless, shameful fruit of sin, ending in death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul asks what fruit (profit) they had from their former sins — the answer being none worth having, only what they are now ashamed of (Barnes, Robertson); the enlightened conscience blushing at the former sins, shame the only present harvest. Henry and Calvin note the terminus: the end of those things is death (eternal ruin). The verse displays the worthless fruit of sin. The verse marks the challenge: what fruit? The verse displays the shame: now ashamed. The verse displays the end: death. The verse displays the harvest: shame and ruin. The worthless fruit displays the emptiness and deadly end of sin. The verse displays the profit: none worth having. The verse displays the shame: of the conscience. The verse displays the terminus: death. The worthless, shameful fruit of sin, ending in death, displays the emptiness and ruin of the old service: the former sins yielded no profit worth having, only present shame to the awakened conscience, and their final end is death. The verse displays the apostle's appeal to the believers' own experience of sin's barrenness and deadly issue, setting off the blessed fruit of the new service (v.22). Thus the apostle drives home the barrenness of sin by an appeal to the conscience itself, which can produce no defense for a service whose only wages were shame and whose only end was death.
22:
"But now being made free from sin, and become servants to God, ye have your fruit unto holiness, and the end everlasting life." The witnesses note the blessed fruit of the new service. On "free from sin, servants to God," the grammatical voices note the new state. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the old: now freed from sin and made God's servants — the happy reversal.

On "fruit unto holiness," the witnesses note the present harvest. The new service yields fruit unto holiness — growth in sanctification, the opposite of the shameful fruit of sin.

On "the end everlasting life," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Cyril note the terminus: the final issue of God's service is everlasting life — over against the death that ends sin's service. The verse displays the blessed fruit: holiness now, everlasting life at the end.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: now freed from sin and made servants to God (the happy reversal of the old state, Barnes, Robertson), the believers have fruit unto holiness (growth in sanctification, the opposite of sin's shameful fruit) and the end everlasting life (over against the death that ends sin's service, Calvin, Cyril). The verse displays the blessed fruit of the new service. The verse marks the state: free from sin, servants to God. The verse displays the fruit: unto holiness. The verse displays the end: everlasting life. The verse displays the contrast: with sin's death. The blessed fruit displays the present holiness and final life of God's service. The verse displays the freedom: from sin. The verse displays the harvest: holiness. The verse displays the terminus: everlasting life. The blessed fruit of the new service — freed from sin, servants to God, fruit unto holiness, the end everlasting life — displays the glorious contrast with the old: where sin yielded shame and death, the service of God yields present holiness and ends in everlasting life. The verse displays the blessed harvest of the believer's changed service, set over against the barren and deadly fruit of sin, and is summed up in the great contrast of wages and gift (v.23).
23:
"For the wages of sin is death; but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord." The witnesses note the great contrast: wages of sin and gift of God. On "the wages of sin is death," the grammatical voices note the desert. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson note "wages" (opsonia) is the soldier's pay — death is the proper, deserved pay that sin earns; not arbitrary but just, what sin merits.

On "the gift of God is eternal life," the witnesses note the contrast. Ellicott and Clarke note the deliberate antithesis: not "wages" but "gift" (charisma) — eternal life is not earned but freely given; sin earns death, but life is God's free gift.

On "through Jesus Christ our Lord," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Reformed Gill note the channel: even this gift comes through Christ — a man merits hell but cannot merit heaven; eternal life is procured by Christ and given freely. The verse displays the great contrast: death the wages of sin, eternal life the gift of God through Christ.

The witnesses agree the verse crowns the chapter with the great contrast: "wages" (opsonia, the soldier's pay) means death is the proper, deserved, just pay that sin earns (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson); but the deliberate antithesis substitutes "gift" (charisma) — eternal life is not earned but freely given (Ellicott, Clarke), and through Jesus Christ our Lord, for a man merits hell but cannot merit heaven (Clarke, Gill). The verse displays the great contrast. The verse marks the wages: death, sin's just pay. The verse displays the gift: eternal life, freely given. The verse displays the antithesis: wages vs gift. The verse displays the channel: through Christ. The great contrast displays the just desert of sin and the free grace of God. The verse displays the desert: death earned. The verse displays the grace: life given. The verse displays the mediator: through Christ. The great contrast — the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord — displays the just desert of sin set against the free grace of God: death is the deserved pay sin earns, but eternal life is not wages but a free gift, procured by and given through Christ. The verse displays one of the most concentrated statements of the gospel, the antithesis of merit and grace, death and life, and crowns the argument of the chapter with the promise of eternal life in Christ.
